schools.summary: Theophylact: offences are hindrances to godly life, the Pharisees their authors; Cyril distinguishes offences against God's glory (heresy) from those wounding the brethren (strife, slander), the latter chiefly in view; offences inevitable yet woe to their cause.

themes: woe unto him through whom they come

schools.summary: Lightfoot/Clarke: the heavy ass-mill stone, drowning an ancient punishment, 'a millstone about the neck' a proverb for an intolerable burden; better such a death than to offend one of the 'little ones' — the weak in faith Christ guards jealously.

themes: offend one of these little ones
the guilt of stumbling the weak
Christ's jealousy for the little ones

schools.summary: Ambrose: forgive the penitent lest he despair; Theophylact: balance faithful rebuke and ready forgiveness — neither harsh upbraiding nor easy overlooking; private friendly correction (Matthew 18:15) shames to repentance where accusation provokes — the discipline of love seeking restoration.

Ellicott: 'against thee' omitted in the best MSS; 'seven times a day' presses the same boundless forgiveness as Matthew's 'seventy times seven' — no accumulation of offences exhausts it; Gill: forgiveness answers to sincere repentance, the disciple not to play suspicious judge.

Theophylact: the hard duties (poverty, avoiding offences) drive the apostles to seek faith, the spring of self-denial; Gregory: a prayer that faith already begun grow to perfection; Lightfoot: perhaps a request to define faith's measure as Christ defined forgiveness.

Barnes: the 'sycamine' = mulberry; Lightfoot: 'mustard seed' a proverb for the smallest measure; the power lies not in the quantity but the genuineness of faith and its almighty object — even the least real faith can do the seemingly impossible, planting a deep-rooted tree in the sea.

themes: faith as a grain of mustard seed
planted in the sea and obey you

schools.summary: Theophylact: faith breeds works, works tempt to pride, so Christ guards the apostles by the servant-parable; Augustine: the servant labouring in this life before the rest to come; the master expects service, not owing the servant ease — the guard against thinking God our debtor.

themes: will he say, sit down to meat

schools.summary: Robertson: the master will require service first; Ellicott connects to Luke 12:37 (there the Master girds to serve the watching servants; here he requires their service) — the disciples' 'meat' to do the Father's will (John 4:34), then share his joy (Revelation 3:20); service before rest.

Robertson: 'does he thank?' expects 'no' — duty done earns no thanks of debt; Ellicott: spoken from the master-slave standpoint to check the self-righteousness ('what shall we have?', Matthew 19:27) creeping into the disciples — obedience establishes no claim of merit on God.

Gill: all Christ's servants to be laborious and continually employed, serving God first; even having done all, to confess 'unprofitable servants' — not that the service is worthless, but it profits God nothing and earns no claim; the death-blow to merit, the reward being of grace not debt.

sides.position: Service that brings God no profit and earns no claim of debt, not that it is worthless or unrewarded

themes: done that which was our duty

schools.summary: Titus of Bostra: Christ passes between the divided Jews and Samaritans to cement both into 'one new man' (Ephesians 2:14-15) — the route foreshadowing the reconciliation of the nations his cross would seal; the breadth of grace, Luke's keynote.

themes: the borderland of Jew and Samaritan

schools.summary: Cyril: the lepers banished and counted unclean by the law; Lightfoot: lepers might consort with lepers (so the ten together, cf. 2 Kings 7:3); 'afar off' as the law required — the leper an emblem of the sinner, sin separating from God, common misery uniting Jew and Samaritan.

themes: leprosy as an emblem of sin

schools.summary: Clarke: one voice from ten, companions in suffering and in prayer; prayer earnest as the need is great — sin the worst leprosy, curable only by Christ; 'Master' (Luke's 'Rabbi'), pleading mercy alone — the humble, believing, importunate cry of faith.

themes: the cry of faith in need

schools.summary: Barnes: the command an implied promise yet a test — sent to the priests (Leviticus 14) while still leprous, with no evidence, requiring faith in his bare word; cleansed 'as they went' (in obedience) — the sinner to obey Christ trusting his power, the blessing met in the path of obedience.

themes: go shew yourselves to the priests
as they went, they were cleansed
blessing in the path of obedience

schools.summary: Barnes: the one, sensible of the mercy, returned first to give thanks (not neglecting the command, which he doubtless fulfilled after) — gratitude the first impulse; the sinner cleansed should first prostrate himself before his Benefactor and glorify God; the loud voice of praise answering the loud cry of prayer.

themes: with a loud voice glorified God

schools.summary: Clarke: the grateful one a Samaritan of corrupt religion, from whom least was expected, vs the nine likely Jews; Ellicott: Luke's catholicity, grace breaking the Jew/Samaritan wall; the prostrate worship hinting at faith in Christ's divinity — covenant privilege no substitute for gratitude.

themes: fell down on his face at his feet

schools.summary: Barnes: the nine literally obeyed yet were selfish and ungrateful, careless of the Benefactor; Christ marks and grieves over their ingratitude ('where are the nine?'); a mirror of human nature — ingratitude the rule, the grateful one the rare exception.

themes: forgetting the Giver in the gift

schools.summary: Clarke: God often gets more praise from those once far off (much forgiven, much love, Luke 7:47) than from the privileged; Ellicott/Robertson: 'stranger' (allogenēs, a term of contempt, no child of Abraham) — only the despised alien returned, rebuking privileged ingratitude; gratitude, not lineage, prized.

themes: returned to give glory to God

schools.summary: Gill: he lay worshipping till Christ bid him rise; all ten 'cleansed,' but to this one alone 'thy faith hath made thee whole' (cf. Luke 7:50) — a deeper blessing, the soul's salvation, beyond the body's cure; gratitude and faith carrying him to the greater gift the thankless nine missed.

debates.question: Does 'made thee whole' add anything beyond the cleansing the nine received?

sides.position: Yes — a deeper blessing, the salvation of the soul, granted to the believing, grateful one

themes: thy faith hath made thee whole

schools.summary: Cyril: the Pharisees ask (perhaps tauntingly) expecting a visible kingdom at Christ's entry to Jerusalem; he answers patiently that the kingdom comes 'not with observation' — not with outward pomp or calculable signs, unlike the Incarnation heralded by prophets and angels; correcting carnal, date-setting expectations.

Barnes gives two readings of 'within you': (1) the inward spiritual reign in the heart, not outward pomp; (2) 'among you / in your midst' — the kingdom already present in Christ the King and his work; both deny the kingdom is a locatable, observable thing ('lo here, lo there'), rebuking the false Christs.

debates.question: Does 'the kingdom of God is within you' mean 'in the heart' or 'in your midst'?

sides.position: In the heart — the inward spiritual reign
Among you / in your midst — present in Christ the King

themes: the kingdom of God is within you

schools.summary: Cyril/Theophylact: Christ forewarns the disciples of coming persecution after his departure — when, exposed to perils and brought before kings, they would long for one of the peaceful 'days of the Son of man' when they walked with him, and not see it; preparing them, yet pointing to his return.

themes: desire to see one of the days of the Son of man
longing for the days of his presence

schools.summary: Gill/Barnes: 'see here, see there' the cry of the false Christs before Jerusalem's fall (Josephus records many), promising deliverance; Christ commands 'follow them not' — since the true coming is sudden and universal (next verse), any localized claim is by that mark false.

Robertson: the second coming sudden and universally visible like lightning, leaving no room for a secret 'here/there' coming; Ellicott on Luke's vivid form ('the two parts under heaven,' 'the day of the Son of man'); the figure suiting both Jerusalem's judgment and the final coming.

themes: so shall the Son of man be

schools.summary: Robertson: the second coming only after the cross; Ellicott: Luke's distinctive interposition of the Passion into the Advent discourse; Clarke: the desolation awaits the nation's filling the cup by rejecting and crucifying Christ — the cross the appointed way to the coming glory, for King and disciple.

Bede: the coming likened to Noah's and Lot's days of sudden destruction; Chrysostom: the antediluvians' unbelief sprang from self-indulgence (desires shaping expectations); Ambrose: not eating/marriage condemned but immoderate, godless absorption — the world careless and unprepared, as at Jerusalem and the final coming.

themes: as in the days of Noe

schools.summary: Gill: not moderate eating but luxurious self-indulgence, putting away Noah's warning; Clarke: lives wholly for this world, no provision for the soul (so at Jerusalem); Ellicott: the continuous tense (habitual absorption), 'flood' always of Noah's deluge — careless security swept away suddenly and totally.

Gill/Barnes: the days of Lot in Sodom, the same careless absorption now extended to commerce and building (buying, selling, planting, building) — men planning for a long future, busy as if nothing would happen; Sodom's overthrow a perpetual proof that God punishes the guilty (Jude 7), heedless of judgment.

themes: as in the days of Lot

schools.summary: Gill: Lot plucked out by the angels before the fire; Clarke: 'Jehovah rained from Jehovah out of heaven' (Genesis 19:24), read by many as a hint of plurality in the Godhead; Ellicott on 'fire and brimstone' (LXX, recurring in Revelation) — sudden total ruin for the wicked, the righteous first delivered.

themes: the same day Lot went out

schools.summary: Gill: as Noah's and Lot's days, so the careless security before Jerusalem's fall and before the final judgment; the Christians delivered (withdrawing to Pella) as Noah and Lot were — the double reference (Jerusalem and the end), the same pattern: careless world, sudden judgment, righteous delivered.

debates.question: Does 'the day the Son of man is revealed' refer to Jerusalem's fall or the final judgment?

sides.position: Both — a double reference, the same pattern in each

themes: the day the Son of man is revealed

schools.summary: Ambrose/Augustine read the housetop as the height of spiritual life, the 'stuff'/'vessels' the carnal senses — let the spiritual man not descend again to the world, nor the labourer in the field look back on renounced worldly prospects; the literal urgency of the flight from Jerusalem (fulfilled in the Christians' escape).

outliers.note: Reads the housetop as spiritual elevation and the household 'vessels' as the carnal senses, warning the spiritual man not to descend to worldly life.

themes: renouncing the world for the soul

schools.summary: Gill: she looked back (curiosity, or yearning after her daughters/Sodom) and became a pillar of salt (Genesis 19:26); Barnes: she looked back, delayed, perhaps wished to save something, made a monument of displeasure — the soul fleeing judgment must forsake all and not look back (cf. Luke 9:62), lest, nearly saved, it perish.

themes: the doom of the divided heart

schools.summary: Ellicott: the better text — 'purchase' his lower life at the price of the higher (a losing bargain), vs 'give life to it' (gain a higher life); Gill: seeking to save life by an earthly refuge loses it; the self-preserving lose, the self-surrendering gain — clinging forfeits, surrender secures the true life.

themes: seek to save his life shall lose it

schools.summary: Theophylact/Cyril: the division is by faith and election, not outward condition — not all rich perish nor poor saved; the bed a symbol of worldly ease, of two at rest one elect is taken, the other left; Ambrose reads the 'night' of the dark hour of trial through which Christ shines like lightning — a great sundering of those outwardly alike.

debates.question: What divides the one taken from the one left?

sides.position: Inward faith and election, not outward condition (rich/poor)

schools.summary: Robertson/Gill: two women at one hand-mill, the humblest daily labour, again divided — outward nearness no security; Cyril of Jerusalem's pastoral comfort: the Judge is no respecter of persons, not despising the poor woman at the mill — the lowliest believer taken, judgment reaching every station.

Gill: two men labouring in the field, companions in toil, divided at the coming; Cyril of Jerusalem: the angels take the faithful husbandman as readily as the landowner — 'He who took the form of a servant despises not servants'; the third figure (bed, mill, field) completing the universal, searching division by faith, not station.

Gill: the disciples ask 'where?'; Barnes: judgment gathers where guilt is greatest, as eagles to prey — pointing to Jerusalem (the carcass) and the Roman armies (the eagles, Rome's standard); Clarke: the birds of prey to the carcass; judgment moral, falling infallibly where corruption is — suiting both Jerusalem and the final judgment.

themes: where the body is, the eagles gather
